posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
2020 Laurent Tribut Chablis - France, Burgundy, Chablis (10/27/2025)
First time with this producer...this wine is showing a riper, tropical side to the fruit...yellow peach, pineapple, sauteed apple...PLENTY of fresh squeeze lemon tang and cut though...some wood spice, dried honey...little edge to it...some roughness, but I kind of like it? But what I REALLY dig in this, is the salty, leesy, wet crushed limestone minerality! Totally Burgundy to me! Throw in a little sea spray...some floral lime blossoms...dry extract...this is a SUPER nice village wine here! I'd drink this all day long! (93 points)

Posted from CellarTracker

IMG_79593024×4032 2.4 MB

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked: You didn’t pick any greenness? I’ve avoided 2020 Chablis pretty much because so many wines have shown vegetal qualities to me, including this very bottling.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Otto_Forsberg:

You didn’t pick any greenness?

You are getting me all hot and bothered!!!

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked: Not at all. Had a few 2020, a Duplessis Vauguraut just the other day…no green notes, but did notice a little richer fruit in each. Maybe my lime blossom, is your veggie? Both wines outstanding to me.

posts.name: Steven Dilley
posts.cooked: I’m a fan in general, but a couple of recent bottles ('18s) have been too leesy, cheesy, lactic for me.

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked: Haven’t had one in several years but, yeah, very solid, classic producer. Some mention on the board but deserving of more attention.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: I think I know the character you mean, but it hasn’t bothered me. What do you think caused it?

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked: IMO this is only an issue lower down the producer hierarchy. For folks that accurately judged picking dates and hand harvest, 2020 is a top tier Chablis vintage. Raveneau, Dauvissat, Fevre, and Samuel Billaud all knocked it out of the park. Although some will disagree I give 2020 Chablis the edge to 19 and 17 - best since 14 IMO. Great acidity which goes a long way for my palate.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Rauno_E_NZ:

I think I know the character you mean, but it hasn’t bothered me. What do you think caused it?

Since it was a quite warm and very dry vintage, I suspect underripeness due to vines shutting down.

I’ve understood that when the grapes were harvested in 2020, the fruit in many parts of Chablis had accumulated relatively good sugar ripeness (ie. levels of potential alcohol were getting relatively high and the levels of acidity were starting to go down) even though physiological ripeness was still not fully there yet (grapes hadn’t had enough time to develop full range of aromatic precursors and/or some grapes were still showing small quantities of methoxypyrazines).

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked: I recall that the 2020 Raveneau Montee de Tonnerre I had last year in a restaurant was OK to drink for me but there was something that seemed not quite right and wasn’t up to par with most Raveneau I’ve had through the years. I’m not sure what it is but, I kind of now may be getting a sense of what Otto is referring to.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Tend to agree, honestly. Maybe I just bought the right wines, but everything in my cellar is just fine. If you are going for early-picked + machine-harvested offerings, then the story might be different, but herbaceousness is only going to be one problem among several.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
My experiences with 2020 Chablis have been limited to these wines:
Alice et Olivier De Moor Chablis 1er Cru Vau de Vey - blind, was green
Jean-Paul & Benoit Droin Chablis 1er Cru Montée de Tonnerre - blind, was noticeably green
Jean-Paul & Benoit Droin Chablis Grand Cru Hommage à Louis - a tiniest bit of greenness, didn’t really bother me
Alain Geoffroy Chablis 1er Cru Fourchaume - a tiny bit of greenness
Laurent Tribut Chablis - was green; poured this blind and other people identified as 2020 Chablis based on the greenness

Don’t know how early-pickers these producers are or if they use machine harvesting, but my (rather small) experience with the 2020 vintage hasn’t been particularly encouraging.

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
LOVE this wine! The edge is gone…the ripeness is tame with salty mineral cut…dry extract and iodine…suckin on sour tarts by the sea! 7 bottles down…Justin I need more!

posts.name: David Griffith
posts.cooked:
I came across this at my local Pavillons in Seal Beach, and bought all twelve bottles

Drank through most of them, and they never disappointed!

I’ve been back many times in the 2 1/2 years since, but never saw another bottle again
